evidence_quotes.quote: When playing these games and buying the extra content required in order to be able to enjoy the game,
do video games need to die (I use this term because they become 100 % inaccessible) also?

paragraphs.text: I really support this initiative and would like to see a reality as I have recently played 2 service games: Destiny 2 and Diablo IV When playing these games and buying the extra content required in order to be able to enjoy the game, I have told me that the two games can perfectly exist if they were completely offline and only one player, considering this really worrying that I ever wanted to play a game to which you are offered $100 extra and who simply do not have access because the company decided to close it. I am not only materially losing my $100 but also the time spent on them. I am a person who too often plays the same game and at different stages of my life, so that the idea that somewhere you want to play something and cannot do so because it is beyond my will really has a negative impact on the idea of wanting to play a Videogame. The mere fact of knowing that at some point no longer existed would remove the motivation of the person wishing to enter a game. Human beings die, but do video games need to die (I use this term because they become 100 % inaccessible) also? I hope at the heart that this initiative will change the world of not only video games but also consumer protection as the same companies treat players as Andean wallets and blaming consumers for their own mistakes (e.g. Ubisoft saying that it is the fault of people that their games fail and when they ask the public to destroy the game disc, this is completely against the law). If approved, it would be very good if this petition were to be brought back into law and even internationally in some future, and it is of absolute benefit to all those who do not consume this type of product. Thank you for Leer.
